ABSTRACT
Accurate forecasting of buildings ’ energy demand is essential for building operators to manage loads and re -
sources efficiently, and for grid operators to balance local production with demand. However, nowadays models 
still struggle to capture nonlinear relationships influenced by external factors like weather and consumer 
behavior, assume constant variance in energy data over time, and often fail to model sequential data. To address 
these limitations, we propose a hybrid Transformer-based model with Liquid Neural Networks and learnable 
encodings for building energy forecasting. The model leverages Dense Layers to learn non-linear mappings to 
create embeddings that capture underlying patterns in time series energy data. Additionally, a Convolutional 
Neural Network encoder is integrated to enhance the model ’ s ability to understand temporal dynamics through 
spatial mappings. To address the limitations of classic attention mechanisms, we implement a reservoir pro -
cessing module using Liquid Neural Networks which introduces a controlled non-linearity through dynamic 
reservoir computing, enabling the model to capture complex patterns in the data. For model evaluation, we 
utilized both pilot data and state-of-the-art datasets to determine the model ’ s performance across various 
building contexts, including large apartment and commercial buildings and small households, with and without 
on-site energy production. The proposed transformer model demonstrates good predictive accuracy and training 
time efficiency across various types of buildings and testing configurations. Specifically, SMAPE scores indicate a

ANN models while the higher R² values further confirm the model’s reliability in capturing energy time series

1. Introduction
The decarbonization of the smart grid requires not only the large- 
scale deployment of renewable energy sources but also better planning 
and demand-side management [ 1 , 2 ]. Therefore, the grid operators have 
put in place demand management programs to encourage consumers to 
reduce or shift energy usage during peak periods. As a significant 
amount of the energy demand is attributed to the buildings, efforts must 
be committed to increase their interoperability with the smart grid and 
transform them into active players participating in demand response 
programs [ 3 , 4 ]. However, to make this vision possible it is important to 
have accurate predictions of the building’s energy demand. They will 
enable the building operators to anticipate the load and efficiently 
allocate resources to achieve cost reductions, and the system operators 
to plan and balance the local production with the demand [ 5 ].
Statistical methods such as Autoregressive Integrated Moving 
Average (ARIMA) and its derivates rely only on the past energy values 
making them relatively easy to interpret and effective in linear contexts 
[ 6–8 ]. However, they have problems in capturing nonlinear relation -
ships in energy consumption patterns, which can be influenced, among 
others, by external factors such as weather variability and consumer 
behavior. Additionally, these models assume that the variance in energy 
data remains constant over time, which may not always be true. 
Therefore, machine learning models have been used for energy pre -
dictions as they excel in capturing complex, nonlinear patterns in data 
and may easily integrate external variables such as weather data [ 9 ]. 
Energy prediction models based on Support Vector Machines (SVMs) 
[ 10 , 11 ] decision trees [ 12 ] and Random Forests [ 13 , 14 ] have been 
successful for short-term load forecasting, offering improved general -
ization compared to autoregression ones. However, these models cannot 
often model sequential energy data as effectively as deep learning 
techniques.
Deep learning models can handle multiple inputs, (i.e. time series 
energy data, weather, consumer behavior), without the need for manual 
feature engineering while addressing the challenge of sequential data 
processing (e.g. Recurrent Neural Networks - RNNs) [ 15 , 16 ]. However, 
RNNs often suffer from the vanishing gradient problem, limiting their 
effectiveness over long energy data sequences [ 17 , 18 ]. Long Short-Term 
Memory (LSTM) models incorporate gating mechanisms to capture 
long-term dependencies in energy data more effectively showing better 
performance in energy demand prediction and capturing temporal dy -
namics like daily and seasonal variations [ 17 ]. Liu et al. [ 19 ] showed 
that LSTM models achieve greater accuracy in electricity load fore -
casting compared to traditional RNNs. Similarly, Bouktif et al. [ 20 ] 
enhance forecasting precision by employing an LSTM model that in -
corporates optimally selected time-lagged features. Sarmas et al. [ 21 ] 
successfully developed and fine-tuned four distinct LSTM models to 
accurately predict the output of three rooftop PV plants in Portugal. 
Additionally, researchers in Michalakopoulos et al. [ 22 ] optimized 
LSTM architectures using the Optuna framework for hyperparameter 
tuning to improve the performance across various PV scenarios.
Recently Transformer models have shown promise in overcoming 
these challenges by using self-attention mechanisms to weigh the 
importance of different parts of the input energy data sequence, allow -
ing them to capture relationships between distant data elements more 
effectively [ 23–25 ]. The multi-head attention mechanism proved to be 
successful in handling complex time-series data scenarios, such as wind 
and solar power forecasting with improved accuracy [ 26 ]. Zhou et al. 
applied this concept in the context of energy forecasting [ 27 ] and their 
Informer model improves the efficiency of Transformers by using 
ProbSparse attention. This enables scalable forecasting for large-scale 
energy datasets without getting an accuracy penalty. Additionally, 
they had introduced positional encoding to make them better suited for 
time series tasks. The multi-head attention mechanism can handle 
long-term dependencies better than traditional RNN-based models like 
LSTM and GRU, obtaining higher accuracy [ 28–30 ]. Also, attention 
mechanisms have been applied to data imputation tasks, being able to 
keep temporal dynamics and improve forecasting accuracy [ 31 ]. 
Furthermore, adaptive models using Principal Component Analysis for 
dimensionality reduction before passing data into the Transformer 
model have been shown to focus only on the most informative features 
[ 32 ]. To enhance the interpretability of the model, the Interformer [ 33 ] 
was proposed for probabilistic short-term forecasting for the residential 
load. By incorporating sparse self-attention, the model reduces the 
computational complexity, while still providing important information 
about the variables that have the highest impact on the predictions. For 
wind energy generation forecasting, Li et al. [ 34 ] have shown that 
integrating the Transformer’s self-attention mechanism with causal 
convolution and empirical mode decomposition improves the fore -
casting accuracy by capturing the local dependencies and the sharp 
variations in wind power data. Similarly, Harrou et al. [ 35 ] integrate a 
Variational Autoencoder with a self-attention mechanism for both the 
encoder and decoder layers. The model enhances the short-term wind 
power prediction accuracy by focusing on the most informative features 
provided in the input and using a nonlinear approximation in modelling 
the energy time series. Yu et al. integrated a dynamic self-attention 
mechanism (i.e., Temporal Fusion Transformer) into a solar power 
forecasting model to improve short-term predictions [ 36 ]. The attention 
mechanism’s dynamic nature enables the model to gradually modify its 
focus on relevant features, hence assisting in the management of the 
various trends monitored in energy generation prediction.
However, the Transformers are more computationally complex than 
traditional neural network models especially as the time series sequence 
length and model size increase. To reduce the computational 
complexity, adaptive mechanisms for encoders, decoders, and attention 
mechanisms have been introduced. Mo et al. [ 37 ] use sparse 
self-attention and temporal pooling to improve computational effi -
ciency, while not compromising the prediction accuracy. They use 
LSTM-based embedding to address the weak temporal prediction capa -
bilities of the transformer model. Adaptive attention mechanisms have 
also been introduced to handle fluctuations in energy data and they 
perform well in terms of medium-term wind power forecasting [ 38 ]. 
Similarly, models that optimize the attention mechanism for 
multi-horizon, multi-source load forecasting in integrated energy sys -
tems, have been shown to improve prediction accuracy [ 39 ]. Wang et al. 
[ 25 ] proposed a cross-entity attention mechanism and shared variable 
selection networks to handle relationships between different entities in a 
smart grid (i.e., buildings, single prosumers, etc.). The mechanism can 
learn relationships among entities to inform the decoder about which 
entity the encoder should focus on. Qu et al. [ 40 ] proposed a transformer 
model with three key features: better long-distance dependence than 
LSTM, a multi-headed attention mechanism, and parallel computation 
for faster training. The model has been successfully applied to forecast 
the power output of multiple wind farms showing better accuracy 
compared to LSTM-based models. Dai et al. [ 41 ] proposed the use of 
self-attention-based neural network to capture the temporal relations in 
power sequences. Such models have shown good adaptation capabilities 
to real-time changes in wind power data being able to handle

ultra-short-term forecasting. Wu et al. [ 42 ], proposed Autoformer which 
adds a decomposition block to describe the trend and seasonal compo -
nents in time-series data, is another significant example. By dividing the 
input data into distinct components, the Autoformer improves fore -
casting accuracy for solar and wind energy.
To further improve the model temporal relationships in sequential 
energy data, generalization capabilities and reduce the computational 
complexity concerning the sequence length, recent studies have 
explored the combination of Transformers with other architectural 
models like CNNs and GRUs. Tian et al. [ 43 ] combine LSTM with 
Informer to improve the representation capabilities in the case of time 
series and a parallel encoder module to improve the robustness. A hybrid 
CNN-GRU-Transformer model has been shown to surpass traditional 
models in terms of accuracy in solar power forecasting [ 44 ]. Also, a 
hybrid CNN-Transformer was proposed using the CNN abilities to 
extract spatial features and combine them with the Transformer ’ s 
attention mechanism [ 45 ]. This hybrid approach shows promising re -
sults in wind power forecasting, improving both accuracy and compu -
tational resource use. Graph-based models have also been employed to 
capture spatial dependencies between different wind farms [ 46 , 47 ]. For 
instance, a Graph-Convolution Network (GCN) combined with 
Multi-Resolution Convolution Networks (MRCN) for short-term wind 
power prediction resulted in higher accuracy, compared to standalone 
CNN or RNN models. The Graph Patch Informer (GPI) extends the 
Transformer model by integrating Graph Attention Networks (GATs) 
that allow for the modeling of spatial dependencies [ 48 ]. Liu and Fu 
applied this hybrid approach to renewable energy forecasting, although 
the model has increased complexity and demands substantial compu -
tational power to train, which limits its scalability for widespread use. 
Additionally, the application of self-supervised techniques can help in 
discovering and learning more features from energy data. Xiang et al. 
proposed a hybrid model combining a Self-Attention Temporal Con -
volutional Network (SATCN) [ 49 ] with an LSTM to predict 
ultra-short-term wind power. This outperforms existing methods like 
LSTM, CNN-LSTM, and TCN-LSTM in terms of root mean square error 
(RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE). Huang et al. demonstrated that 
this approach, when combined with Transformer models, has the po -
tential to increase the generalization capabilities in multi-step fore -
casting tasks [ 50 ]. For residential buildings, a hybrid model combining 
multivariate variational mode decomposition, and a Temporal Fusion 
Transformer has been developed for short load forecasting and it de -
composes the load series into intrinsic mode functions, which makes the 
model handle variations within the input data better [ 51 ]. This, com -
bined with a whale optimization algorithm, results in higher forecasting 
accuracy. Finally, Pan et al. [ 52 ] introduced a transformer framework 
that uses a local-global scale architecture. The model is highly appli -
cable due to the integration of edge computing and IoT devices that 
enable real-time data collection and processing.
Despite their strengths for natural languages processing, the state-of- 
the-art transformers models have specific drawbacks in time series en -
ergy predictions, regarding computation, temporal dynamics and noise 
handling. The computational cost of these models, especially when 
applied to large energy datasets, remains a major drawback [ 53 , 54 ]. 
Wang et al. [ 55 ] note that the high computational requirements for 
multi-source data fusion models may lead to a decline in performance in 
real-time applications. Furthermore, for specific scenarios, LSTMs or 
linear regression can outperform the Transformer model and in envi -
ronments where data is low-dimensional, the marginal accuracy im -
provements do not always justify the computational costs. To address 
these limitations, we propose a hybrid approach that integrates a 
Transformer-based architecture with Liquid Neural Networks (LNNs) 
and learnable encodings to handle both the complex temporal de -
pendencies and non-linear patterns found in energy consumption data. 
The model leverages Dense Layers to learn non-linear mappings, 
enabling it to create embeddings that capture underlying patterns in 
time series energy data. A Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) encoder 
is integrated to enhance the model ’ s ability to understand temporal 
dynamics through spatial mappings. At the same time, an adaptive po -
sitional encoding combines sinusoidal, temporal, and learnable encod -
ings. To address the limitations of classic attention mechanisms, which 
primarily rely on linear transformations and simple dot products and 
may not capture these non-linear relationships effectively, we introduce 
a Reservoir Processing module implemented using LNNs. A controlled 
non-linearity is introduced through dynamic reservoir computing, 
which can model complex patterns in the data that are not immediately 
apparent or are obscured by noise and fluctuations. The proposed 
transformer model shows good prediction accuracy with good training 
time efficiency over various pilot data and state-of-the-art datasets while 
considering different building contexts, such as large buildings of 
apartments or small households with and without on-site energy 
production.
The paper ’ s contributions beyond the state of the art are: 
• A hybrid transformer model for building energy forecasting that 
combines the transformer architecture with LNNs, and learnable 
encodings achieving good prediction accuracy and training effi -
ciency across different building contexts.
• Integration of CNN encoders with adaptive positional encoding to 
capture non-linear mappings and enhance the understanding of 
temporal dynamics in time series energy data.
• A Reservoir Processing module implemented using Liquid Neural 
Networks to capture more effective complex non-linear energy data 
patterns that can be masked by noise and fluctuations.
• Evaluation of predictive performance across different types of 
buildings including apartments building, residential and commercial 
buildings and households prosumers showing good generalization 
features and energy prediction accuracy.
The rest of the paper is organized as follows: Section 2 describes the 
architecture of our proposed hybrid transformer model detailing its key 
components; Section 3 details the experimental setup and energy pre -
diction results for various building cases and Section 4 discuss the pre -
diction accuracy and training time results in comparison with basic 
transformer, LTSM and ANN, whilst Section 5 concludes the paper and 
presents potential future directions.
2. Hybrid transformer model for energy prediction
We have adapted the transformer model to the specific needs of 
energy consumption or production prediction (as illustrated in Fig. 1 ) by 
designing each component to maximize its efficacy in learning from time 
series data. The description of the proposed hybrid model focuses on the 
core building blocks, their interplay, and the rationale behind their se -
lection, emphasizing their contributions and potential limitations within 
the context of time series energy prediction.

Since basic transformer architectures do not inherently process 
sequential data in order, positional encodings are added to the input 
embeddings to provide information about the order of the energy data 
samples. This ensures that our hybrid model understands the temporal 
dynamics of the data. We employ an adaptive positional encoding 
approach, combining sinusoidal and learnable encodings. This method 
leverages the strengths of both types to capture temporal patterns more 
effectively.

encodings, which are added to the input embeddings. The cycle length

A significant drawback of this approach is that the energy con -
sumption data often exhibit non-linear patterns due to various factors 
such as variable consumer behavior, sudden changes in weather, or 
differing responses to price fluctuations. Classic attention mechanisms, 
which primarily rely on linear transformations and simple dot products, 
may not capture these non-linear relationships as effectively. To bypass 
this, we added a Reservoir Processing module implemented using 
Liquid Neural Networks. This introduces a controlled non-linearity 
through its dynamic reservoir computing approach, which can model 
complex patterns in the data that are not immediately apparent or are 
obscured by noise and fluctuations. By processing queries and keys 
through the Liquid Neural Networks, the attention mechanism can 
dynamically adjust how different parts of the sequence influence each 
other. Also, the Liquid Neural Networks can function as a selective 
attention outlet, by offering adaptive attention to more important fea -
tures and reducing convergence times.

After attention processing, the FFN ( Feed-Forward Network ) 
further transforms the attention output. The inclusion of gating mech -
anisms, such as Gated Linear Units (GLUs), allows the model to regulate 
the flow of information through the network, adding an additional layer 
of non-linearity and control. Applied before each sub-layer (attention 
and FFN) and to their outputs, layer normalization helps stabilize the 
neural network ’ s learning process, mitigating issues related to varying 
scales and distribution of layer inputs. Finally, dropout was used as a 
regularization technique, randomly setting a fraction of the input units 
to 0 at each update during training time, which helps prevent overfitting 
by ensuring that no subset of neurons becomes too reliant on each other.

3. Evaluation results
We utilized both pilot data and state-of-the-art datasets to validate 
the model ’ s performance across different building contexts, such as 
large buildings of apartments or small households with and without on- 
site energy production.
For all evaluation cases, we have considered a wide array of features 
each contributing to the predictive performance of the proposed hybrid 
transformer model. Temporal features like the hour of the day, day of 
the week, week of the year, month of the year, and day of the year are 
used to capture patterns in energy consumption. Next, we incorporated 
exogenous variables such as weather data, including outside tempera -
ture, humidity, wind speed, cloud cover, and precipitation amount. They 
directly influence the heating and cooling loads of buildings, which are 
major components of energy consumption. For instance, higher outside 
temperatures increase the demand for air conditioning, while lower 
temperatures increase heating requirements. Humidity levels can affect 
indoor climate control systems, leading to variations in energy usage. To 
understand autocorrelations in buildings ’ energy usage we have used 
lagged values of consumed energy from the last 6-hour window, the 
previous day and week. Statistical features such as moving averages, 
moving medians, exponentially weighted moving averages, and rolling 
standard deviations over 6-hour and 24-hour windows were used to 
assess short-term trends and variability in buildings ’ energy consump -
tion. Additionally, rolling skew and kurtosis measures were used to 
understand changes in the distribution shape of energy consumption 
patterns. Trends in building energy consumption profiles are analyzed 
using the average hourly consumption for each day of the week, the rate 
of change in energy consumption, and components extracted from time 
series decomposition, such as trend, seasonality, and residuals. We have 
included anomaly detection features like outlier indicators, spike iden -
tification, spike magnitude to detect unusual events, and frequency 
features derived from the Fourier Transform of the energy consumption 
signal to capture cyclical patterns in energy consumption. Finally, we 
have explored potential nonlinear relationships between consumption 
and its statistical measures.
From the extended set of features, we selected the specific and 
relevant subset for each evaluation case using the Firefly Algorithm 
[ 56 ]. The algorithm iteratively refined the feature subsets by attracting 
less optimal solutions toward better ones, based on a fitness function, 
ensuring diversity in the search, while converging toward the optimal 
subset of features. This metaheuristic optimization technique permitted 
us to identify the most critical features for each case by maximizing the 
evaluation metric while minimizing redundancy and irrelevant infor -
mation. By employing the feature selection algorithm for each scenario, 
we ensured that each dataset ’ s particularities will be considered, to 
enhance the model ’ s predictive performance. Also, this approach 
improved training efficiency and reduced the risk of overfitting, as it 
allowed the model to focus on the most significant inputs. Moreover, we 
have employed regularization techniques such as early stopping to halt 
training when the performance no longer improves and. model check -
point to save best model parameters during training and resume training 
in case of interruptions. The selection of the hyperparameters was

ensure optimal performance. The selection of a 24-dimensional input

hourly consumption data. The choice was grounded in the daily peri -

guided search, where we tested different values selected from pre -

that resulted in the best trade-off between training time and model ac -
curacy, in all evaluation cases. It is also important to note that to prevent 
overfitting, we relied on a strategy that combined model checkpointing 
to save the best model in terms of accuracy on the validation set and 
early stopping to halt training when validation loss stopped decreasing.
3.1. Building of apartments
We utilized energy consumption data from two residential buildings 
(i.e., 1F and 1B) where students can rent 234 apartments yearly. To 
monitor electricity usage, a Building Energy Management System 
(BEMS) has been installed. MQTT-based data integration is used, where 
producers send data to a queue at a fixed rate. MQTT consumers then 
collect data from the queue and perform the necessary energy conver -
sions. Energy consumption data was collected over an extended period 
throughout the year 2022, spanning from January 1st to December 31st. 
The building is used throughout the whole year, however with different 
energy consumption. Fig. 2 monthly average energy consumption shows 
seasonal trends, with noticeable dips around the summer months (June 
to August), and peaks towards the end of the year (October to 
December). The energy demand in both buildings’ data align, suggesting 
similar usage patterns and shared external factors influencing 
consumption.
Table 2 summarizes statistical metrics for the energy consumption of 
the two buildings. The metrics indicate that the energy consumption 
patterns in both buildings are quite similar, in both range and value.
The feature subset selected for both buildings, as determined by the 
Firefly Algorithm, encompasses comprehensive information about the 
energy consumption patterns. This subset includes exogenous variables 
such as external temperature, temporal dependencies, and methods for 
smoothing short-term fluctuations. The selection process ensures the 
model’s capability to accurately account for daily and weekly usage 
patterns, seasonal trends, and anomalous consumption events, as shown 
in Table 3 .

3.2. Residential building
The residential building selected is in Wiener Neustadt, Austria. The 
building consists of two floors, each equipped with a dedicated PV 
system supplying electricity. The data includes energy consumption data 
from the energy meter of the first floor and energy production data from 
the PV system supplying this floor. The data spans two years with one- 
hour granularity, providing a comprehensive foundation for our ana -
lyzes and subsequent modeling efforts.

for our hybrid transformer model training. Missing and anomalous 
values were meticulously handled: zero and negative consumptions 
were identified, with negative consumptions replaced by the mean of 
neighbouring non-negative values. Specific dates with negative con -
sumptions were excluded, and a similar approach was applied to energy 
production anomalies, such as stuck values. In the case of energy con -
sumption prediction feature engineering was performed to enhance the 
data. Table 5 summarize the selected features identified by the Firefly 
algorithm as the most significant for predictions enabling the simplifi -
cation of the model by focusing on the most impactful variables. The 
features include direct measurements or time-based features that are 
essential inputs into the model. The derived features are calculated 
values such as Z-scores or rolling statistics, which help capture trends or 
anomalies in the data. The interaction features involve the combination 
or interaction of other features to provide deeper insights, such as

variability in energy datasets.
4. Discussion
In this section, we discuss the fundamental differences between the 
main types of models used for building energy prediction such as the 
classical transformer, LTSM and ANN, and our hybrid model (see 
Table 14 ), comparing prediction accuracy and training time across 
various cases.
ANNs rely on relatively simple solutions for modeling relationships 
in time-series energy data, using a straightforward structure of layers 
with interconnected neurons and activation functions. The models are 
computationally efficient but lack mechanisms to process sequential or 
temporal data which are rather important for building ’ s energy pre -
dictions. Therefore, expensive data pre-processing and feature engi -
neering must be employed compensate. LSTMs, on the other hand, are 
designed with mechanisms, such as gated memory cells, which enable 
them to retain information about past states and to model medium-term 
temporal dependencies, especially for time series energy data with low 
variability and consistent patterns. However, the models face several 
challenges that limit their effectiveness in building energy forecasting, 
such as difficulties in sequence modeling, which often require the inte -
gration of other models like CNNs and RNNs. Additionally, the models 
are exposed to the vanishing gradient problem, and their performance 
tends to decrease when confronted with large datasets with noisy data. 
The classical transformer models rely on self-attention mechanisms and 
positional encodings to efficiently represent the relationships over long 
energy time series sequences. This is achieved by dynamically assigning 
weighs to different time instances in the series, allowing the transformer 
model to focus on the most important and relevant elements of the input 
energy data sequence. Positional encodings, on the other hand, provide 
the model with temporal information by capturing dependencies among 
distant time steps, making them suitable for building energy predictions. 
However, they face limitations in computational efficiency, being more 
resource-dependent, and struggle with high energy data variability, 
especially in the case of small-scale energy data. Additional mechanisms 
are needed to better understand the nonlinearity in building ’ s energy 
consumption and capture finer grain temporality more efficiently. Our 
hybrid transformer model addresses the challenges exposed above by 
integrating adaptive encoding, reservoir processing and LNNs. The 
adaptive encoding combines sinusoidal, learnable and temporal 
encodings for better representation of temporal patterns and capturing 
complex temporal within the building ’ s energy data. The inclusion of 
LNNs introduces controlled non-linearity, enabling the model to better 
capture complex relationships between input elements, and to reduce 
the computational overhead of our transformer model. Finally, the CNN

To benchmark the energy prediction accuracy of our hybrid trans -
former model, we compared it against LSTM, a basic transformer, and 
ANN. All models were trained using sequences of 24-hour energy 
consumption data for the various building cases discussed in Section 3 , 
and then used to predict the next 24 energy values (see Table 15 ). The 
models were optimized using techniques such as model checkpoints, 
learning rate reduction, and early stopping to prevent overfitting and 
enhance generalization. The LTSM model features three layers with

decreasing units (40, 20, and 10), followed by a dense layer to produce 
the energy prediction results. The LSTM showed a higher median sMAPE 
and lower R² compared to the proposed method, indicating less accurate 
and reliable predictions. The LSTM model demonstrated some capability 
in capturing temporal dependencies, as in the case of Portugal house -
holds that exhibit consistent energy consumption patterns. However, the 
LSTM models struggled with the variability and complexity inherent in 
energy consumption patterns. The ANN model features a structure with 
three Dense layers, with 120, 60 and 30 neurons, with ReLU as activa -
tion function. ANN performed well in capturing simple linear patterns 
and indicated a degree of computational efficiency on small datasets 
with lower variability, as observed in the residential buildings and 
households’ test cases. For residential buildings, ANN prediction model 
resulted in an sMAPE of 10.22 %, while in households it recorded an 
sMAPE of 10.04 %, comparable with the other models. However, the 
model failed to consider the more complex patterns within the con -
sumption data, leading to suboptimal performance in scenarios with 
high variability, such as in apartment buildings, where sMAPE increased 
to 21.42 %, in comparison to 9.64 % obtained by the hybrid transformer 
model.
The basic transformer uses sinusoidal non-cyclical positional 
encoding, struggled with prediction accuracy in several cases. This was 
evident for building test cases with longer input sequences due its reli -
ance on static encodings that limited its adaptability. In the apartment 
buildings case, the prediction model has lower accuracy (i.e., 20.11 % 
sMAPE), compared to our hybrid transformer (i.e., 9.64 sMAPE). The 
model is less capable of identifying periods with sudden changes in 
energy data, as demonstrated in households cases, where MAE increased 
to 78.6 from 44.8 and R
2 
decreased from 0.91 to 0.81. The basic 
transformer model could not interpret all the variance in the data, 
especially periods with peaks or valleys. Moreover, it achieved worse 
accuracy results compared to the LSTM model and, in some cases, 
underperformed even the ANN. Our hybrid transformer model demon -
strated advantages over all above models in terms of accuracy. The 
integration of LNNs and learnable encodings allows for better manage -
ment of complex relationships and fluctuations in building energy data, 
as evidenced by its error metrics in apartment buildings (i.e., sMAPE of 
9.64 %) and households (i.e., sMAPE of 9.26 %). The CNN encoder 
enhanced the model’s ability to process the locality in the energy data 
inputs, improving the capturing of temporal analysis features, particu -
larly in commercial buildings, with an improvement of approximately 
25 % than the next model in terms of sMAPE. Additionally, our model 
surpassed the ANN and LSTM in terms of accuracy in all test cases, 
confirming its superiority in handling diverse load consumption sce -
narios, even though it struggled with predictions during periods of 
abrupt changes in the consumption patterns.
We compared the training times of all the considered models to 
evaluate the trade-offs between accuracy and time overhead for building 
energy prediction. The proposed hybrid transformer model is more 
computationally intensive than simpler architectures like LSTM and 
ANN, however it improves the prediction accuracy for all building cases 
considered. ANN is the most computationally efficient due to its 
simplicity leading to shorter training times, which makes them suitable 
for scenarios in which resources are constrained. However, it provides 
less accurate energy prediction results in all building cases than both 
LSTM and the proposed model, as it can be seen in Table 16 . LSTM 
achieved better prediction accuracy than ANN in all test cases, partic -
ularly for apartment buildings, with a 23.81 % improvement in sMAPE, 
and for commercial buildings, with a 13 % improvement. However, 
despite their relatively fast training times, compared to transformer- 
based models, LSTMs are impacted by the vanishing gradient problem. 
Consequently, their predictive accuracy can be limited, even though 
their computational efficiency represents an advantage, as evidenced by 
the shorter training times. The basic transformer model are computa -
tionally expensive due to the quadratic complexity of their attention 
mechanism and the need for multiple stacked layers. Training trans -
former models takes significantly longer than LSTM and ANN (on 
average, 3.5 higher training time than LSTM and 6.5 higher training 
time than ANN), but generates better prediction accuracy results in few 
situations not sufficiently justify the need for such intensive computa -
tional needs. The hybrid Transformer model follows the trend of having 
longer training times than the traditional methods, but despite this, it 
improves significantly the energy prediction accuracy. On average, the 
proposed model shows an improvement of approximately 26.3 % in 
training time compared to the basic transformer, showcasing the 
advantage of the hybrid architecture in both accuracy and resource ef -
ficiency, making it relevant for buildings energy demand forecasting. 
This makes the hybrid transformer model suitable for applications 
where building energy prediction accuracy is prioritized over the 
training time and resources overhead. LSTM models, on the other hand, 
offer a balance between accuracy and computational efficiency. The 
ANN models, despite being the fastest, show slightly reduced precision, 
making them more appropriate when rapid predictions are needed and 
some compromise on accuracy is acceptable.
The proposed hybrid transformer model presents important gener -
alization features across various types of buildings (i.e., apartment 
buildings, household prosumers, and commercial buildings), showing 
good energy prediction accuracy. Several measures can be implemented 
to enhance its generalization capabilities further and extend its appli -
cability to other types of energy assets. To better account for the energy

different time series granularities. In the experiments mentioned above, 
all building energy data had one-hour granularity. The encoding module 
requires adjustments to capture both medium-term and long-term de -
pendencies. In the current version, the cyclical part of sinusoidal

be achieved by integrating domain-specific information, such as

scaling factor across all queries and keys. Based on the variability within 
the energy data, the number of attention heads can also be adjusted for 
each building scenario.
5. Conclusions and future work
In this work, we developed a hybrid transformer model, integrating 
LNN, CNN, and learnable encoding to forecast the energy consumption 
of various types of buildings. The CNNs were introduced for input se -
quences processing to enhance feature representations, for the model to 
better capture spatial and local dependencies, before moving to tem -
poral analysis. Additionally, LNN and learnable encodings, provide a 
non-linear component into the model architecture addressing the high 
variability and long temporal dependencies in energy consumption data. 
A Reservoir Processing module implemented using Liquid Neural Net -
works is used to capture more effective complex non-linear energy data 
patterns that can be masked by noise and fluctuations.
The experimental results show that our model achieved better ac -
curacy compared to basic transformer, LTSM and ANN and faster 
convergence times than basic transformer. Moreover, it shows good 
generation features as we have utilized the model on both pilot data and 
state-of-the-art datasets storing the energy consumption of different 
building contexts, such as large buildings of apartments, commercial 
building or small households with and without on-site energy produc -
tion. However, the model showed some limitations during periods with 
abrupt changes in energy usage. These results suggest that further 
refinement is necessary to efficiently handle these outlier patterns. 
Furthermore, the hybrid transformer models require more computa -
tional resources than traditional models such as LTSM and ANN, 
although convergence time was reduced due to the addition of LNNs, 
compared to basic transformer. However, despite the need for additional 
resources, the overall improvement in terms of prediction accuracy 
metrics is significant making it a useful tool for both building and grid 
operators.
Regarding future work, we aim to improve the model’s capacity to 
handle anomalies and outlier patterns by including supplementary fac -
tors such as socio-economic influences and consumer behaviors and 
anomaly detection techniques to manage irregularities in energy data. 
Methods need to be developed to tailor the input energy data to mini -
mize the impact of outliers or better consider for anomalous energy 
behaviors. Data augmentation can enhance the model’s ability to 
generalize by simulating a wider range of scenarios, including anomalies 
in sensor data and extreme energy usage patterns. Various simulation 
techniques can be employed to evaluate extreme cases that are under -
represented in the dataset while adding random noise can help simulate 
sensor inaccuracies. Finally, the hybrid transformer’s attention 
mechanisms can be enhanced to more effectively handle sudden changes 
or anomalies in energy usage. Attention mechanisms can be combined 
with physics-informed models to consider physics principles and 
domain-specific knowledge for building energy demand prediction, 
could further improve the model’s adaptability and generalization ca -
pacity. Furthermore, it may improve its ability to capture the relation -
ship between physical phenomena and building energy demand.
